skill.name: Risk Manager
skill.description: Monitor portfolio risk, R-multiples, and position limits. Creates hedging strategies, calculates expectancy, and implements stop-losses.
skill.summary: A financial risk management skill for portfolio protection and systematic trade analysis.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a prompt-only skill with no executable code. Static analysis scanned 0 files and detected 0 potential security issues with a risk score of 0/100. The skill provides guidance and templates for financial risk management without any code execution, network access, or file system operations. No security concerns identified.

content.user_title: Build systematic portfolio risk management strategies
content.value_statement: Traders struggle with inconsistent risk measurement and emotional decision-making. This skill provides systematic R-multiple tracking, position sizing, and hedging strategies for disciplined portfolio protection.
actual_capabilities: Calculate position sizes using Kelly criterion and risk percentages
Track trades in R-multiples for consistent performance analysis
Compute Value at Risk (VaR) and correlation matrices
Generate hedging strategies using options and futures
Perform stress testing with Monte Carlo simulations
limitations: Provides guidance only - does not execute actual trades
Calculations require accurate user-provided input data
Does not access real-time market data or pricing feeds
Recommendations should be validated with professional financial advice

use_cases.title: Portfolio Risk Assessment
use_cases.description: Generate comprehensive risk reports analyzing position exposure, correlation risks, and portfolio-level Value at Risk. Identify concentration risks and recommend diversification strategies.

use_cases.title: Trade Performance Tracking
use_cases.description: Track all trades in R-multiple terms to calculate win rate, average win/loss, and overall expectancy. Remove emotional bias from performance evaluation.

use_cases.title: Hedging Strategy Design
use_cases.description: Create protective hedging strategies using options, futures, or inverse positions. Calculate hedge ratios and cost-benefit analysis for portfolio insurance.
use_cases.target_user: Portfolio managers protecting against downside risk

prompt_templates.title: Basic Position Sizing
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate the appropriate position size for a trade where I have a $10,000 account, want to risk 2% per trade, my entry is $50, and my stop-loss is $45.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner trader learning position sizing fundamentals

prompt_templates.title: R-Multiple Trade Tracking
prompt_templates.prompt: I have 5 closed trades with the following results in R: +2.5R, -1R, +0.5R, +3R, -1R. Calculate my win rate, average win, average loss, and overall expectancy.

prompt_templates.title: Correlation Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the correlation risk in my portfolio containing: SPY (40%), QQQ (30%), IWM (20%), and TLT (10%). Identify concentration risks and suggest diversification improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Hedging Strategy with Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a protective put strategy for my $500,000 equity portfolio currently at risk from market volatility. Calculate the cost of 5% downside protection for 3 months using SPY puts.

output_examples.input: Account: $25,000, Risk per trade: 1.5%, Entry: $120, Stop: $115
output_examples.output: Position Size: 75 shares | Capital at Risk: $375 (1.5%) | Dollar Risk per Share: $5 | Total Position Value: $9,000 (36% of account)
Win Rate: 57% (4/7) | Avg Win: 2.63R | Avg Loss: -1R | Expectancy: +1.07R per trade | With 60 trades/year at 1R=$500: Expected annual return ~$32,100

best_practices: Always define maximum risk per trade (1-2% of account) before entering positions
Track every trade in R-multiples to enable objective performance analysis
Use systematic stop-losses and never move them further away after entry
anti_patterns: Increasing position size after losses to recover quickly (gambler's fallacy)
Removing stop-losses or widening them when trades move against you
Ignoring correlation risk by holding multiple positions with identical exposures

faq.question: What is an R-multiple and why should I use it?
faq.answer: An R-multiple expresses profit or loss relative to your initial risk (1R = maximum loss on a trade). Using R-multiples normalizes performance across different position sizes and enables objective strategy comparison.

faq.question: How do I calculate optimal position size?
faq.answer: Position Size = (Account Value × Risk %) ÷ (Entry Price - Stop Price). For a $10,000 account risking 2% with a $5 stop distance, you would buy 40 shares ($200 risk ÷ $5 per share).

faq.question: What is the Kelly criterion?
faq.answer: The Kelly criterion calculates optimal bet size as: K% = W - (1-W)/R, where W is win rate and R is win/loss ratio. Most traders use half-Kelly to reduce volatility while maintaining positive expectancy.

faq.question: How often should I rebalance my portfolio?
faq.answer: Review correlations and position weights monthly or after significant market moves. Rebalance when any position deviates more than 25% from target weight or when correlations increase materially.

faq.question: What is Value at Risk (VaR)?
faq.answer: VaR estimates the maximum expected loss over a specific time period at a given confidence level. A 95% one-day VaR of $10,000 means there is a 5% chance of losing more than $10,000 in one day.

faq.question: When should I use hedging strategies?
faq.answer: Hedge when portfolio risk exceeds your tolerance, during known high-volatility events (earnings, Fed meetings), or when you want to maintain long positions while protecting against short-term downside.
